title.en: What is YAML? Complete Guide to YAML Syntax and Use Cases

description.en: A complete guide to YAML — syntax, data types, anchors and aliases, comparison with JSON and TOML, and common use cases in CI/CD, Kubernetes, and application configuration.

intro.en: YAML (YAML Ain't Markup Language, a recursive acronym) is a human-readable data serialisation standard. It was designed to be easy for humans to read and write, making it the preferred format for configuration files in DevOps tooling, CI/CD pipelines (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI), container orchestration (Kubernetes, Docker Compose), and application configuration frameworks.

content.en: YAML uses indentation (spaces, not tabs) to denote structure. Key-value pairs are written as key: value on separate lines. Lists use a dash (- item) for each item. Strings do not require quotes unless they contain special characters. Comments start with #. A YAML document starts with --- (optional) and ends with ... (optional). YAML is a superset of JSON — every valid JSON document is also valid YAML, making migration between the two formats straightforward.

YAML supports strings, integers, floats, booleans (true/false/yes/no/on/off), null (null/~), and dates (2024-01-15). It also supports multi-line strings using the | (literal block) or > (folded block) indicators. Block scalars with | preserve newlines; folded blocks with > convert newlines to spaces. YAML's type detection is implicit — the value 42 is parsed as an integer and true as a boolean without quotes. To force a value to be treated as a string, wrap it in quotes.

YAML's anchors (&name) and aliases (*name) allow values to be defined once and reused throughout the document, reducing duplication. The merge key (<<:) allows one mapping to be merged into another. These features are particularly powerful in CI/CD configurations where the same steps, environment variables, or settings appear in multiple places. No other common data format (JSON, TOML) supports this kind of in-document reference.

YAML is the configuration language of choice for: GitHub Actions (.github/workflows/*.yml), GitLab CI/CD (.gitlab-ci.yml), Kubernetes manifests (deployments, services, configmaps), Docker Compose (docker-compose.yml), Ansible playbooks, Helm charts, OpenAPI/Swagger API specifications, and many application frameworks (Ruby on Rails' database.yml, Spring Boot's application.yml). Its human-readable syntax and comment support make it ideal for files that developers read and write directly.

YAML's implicit typing can cause surprises. The value yes is parsed as boolean true, which can break configuration values like country codes ('NO' for Norway). The value 1.0 is a float; 01 is an octal integer in YAML 1.1 (a common source of bugs with port numbers). Tabs are forbidden for indentation. Special characters in strings (:, #, {, [) require quoting. These gotchas have led to jokes about YAML being 'Yet Another Minefield for Aspiring Linguists' and prompted projects to switch to TOML for simpler configs.

question.en: Is YAML the same as JSON?

answer.en: YAML and JSON represent the same fundamental data structures (mappings, sequences, scalars) but with very different syntax. YAML is more human-readable and supports comments and anchors; JSON is simpler, has faster parsers, and is more widely used in APIs. Valid JSON is valid YAML, so you can always convert between the two.

question.en: Why is YAML used for Kubernetes?

answer.en: Kubernetes chose YAML for its manifests because YAML is more readable than JSON for long configuration documents, supports comments for documentation, and allows multi-document files (multiple --- separated documents in one file). The tradeoff is YAML's complexity and pitfalls, which have prompted community tools like kustomize and Helm to generate YAML programmatically.

question.en: How do I convert YAML to JSON?

answer.en: Use our YAML to JSON converter tool — paste your YAML and get formatted JSON instantly in your browser, with no server upload. In code: Python's PyYAML library (yaml.safe_load + json.dumps), Node.js's js-yaml library (yaml.load + JSON.stringify), or the yq command-line tool.

question.en: Why can YAML have comments but JSON cannot?

answer.en: YAML was designed as a human-writable configuration format, so comments were included as a first-class feature. JSON was designed as a minimal data interchange format for machine parsing, and its creator deliberately excluded comments to prevent config-directive abuse. If you need comments in a JSON-like format, use JSONC or TOML instead.
